description: The world's most remarkable urban transformation story — a city once synonymous with danger that has become a model of innovation, with eternal spring weather, a roaring digital nomad scene, and cable cars connecting hilltop communities to the city below.

neighborhoodRecs: El Poblado — Expat and tourist hub, safe, walkable, excellent restaurants and bars, concentrated nightlife, Parque Lleras.
Laureles — More local neighbourhood, great coffee shops and restaurants, residential feel, more authentic than El Poblado.
Envigado — Just south of El Poblado, ultra-local, incredible cheap food (bandeja paisa), very safe, off the tourist trail.
Armenia (near Centro) — Emerging creative district, not for first-time visitors but interesting for longer stays.
areasToAvoid: Comunas 1, 8, 13 (except official tours) — gang territories in some sectors, don't venture without a guided tour.
Downtown Centro Histórico after dark — petty crime is common at night in the main plaza area.
Accepting drinks from strangers at bars — scopolamine (burundanga) drug spiking is a real risk.
scamWarnings: Scopolamine (devil's breath) drug: never leave your drink unattended and be wary of overly friendly strangers offering drinks or cigarettes.
Fake police checks: real Colombian police don't stop tourists to check wallets on the street. Walk away or go to the nearest restaurant.
Taxi overcharging from airport: always use InDriver or Cabify apps, or the official taxi booth inside the airport terminal.
Pablo Escobar tours: while popular, some operators exploit victims' families. Research the operator's ethics before booking.

airportTransfer.cheapest: Metro + cable car combo from Aeropuerto station to El Poblado — COP $2,950 (~$0.75), takes 45–60 min. Great experience.
airportTransfer.fastest: Uber/Cabify from José María Córdova airport — COP $40,000–60,000 (~$10–15), takes 35–45 min.
airportTransfer.recommended: Cabify or InDriver app for reliable, transparent pricing. Uber is available but app-only taxi policy in Colombia is worth knowing.
airportTransfer.taxiEstimate: Official airport taxi COP $55,000–75,000 to El Poblado. Agree on the price before departing or use the meter.

tippingEtiquette: 10% tip at restaurants is standard. It's often added as 'propina voluntaria' on the bill — check if it's already included.
Tip taxi drivers by rounding up: COP $1,000–2,000 on top of the fare.
Street arepa vendors and market stalls: no tipping expected.
Medellín has warm, social culture — 'manejá' and 'bacano' (great, cool) are words that will earn you smiles from locals.
Never joke about Pablo Escobar with locals — it's deeply painful. The transformation is something Paisas are genuinely proud of.

dailyCostBreakdown.accommodation: Hostel dorm COP $25,000–50,000 | Guesthouse COP $80,000–150,000 | Mid-range hotel COP $200,000–400,000
dailyCostBreakdown.meals: Lunch corriente COP $10,000–15,000 | Arepa con todo COP $5,000 | Restaurant dinner COP $40,000–80,000
dailyCostBreakdown.transport: Metro COP $2,950 | Uber COP $8,000–20,000 | Día de campo bike rental COP $15,000
dailyCostBreakdown.activities: Pablo Escobar tour COP $80,000–150,000 | Parque Arví cable car COP $10,000 | Salsa class COP $40,000

packingTips: Light layers — Medellín's 'City of Eternal Spring' means a consistent 22°C. Evenings can be 16°C in the hills.
Rain jacket — rain can come in short bursts throughout the year.
Good walking shoes — El Poblado and Laureles are hilly.
Spanish phrasebook — English is limited outside tourist areas.
premium.bookingStrategy: July–August (dry season) and December–January (festival season) are peak times. Feria de las Flores in August is spectacular — book 2 months ahead. The city is great year-round due to the stable 22°C climate.
localTips: The Medellín Metro is the pride of the city — efficient, clean, and the cable cars to Comunas 13 are a genuine community achievement.
Bandeja paisa (the national dish) at a local restaurante corriente costs COP $12,000–18,000 and will defeat most appetites.
Parque Lleras in El Poblado is the nightlife hub. Go on Thursday for locals, Friday/Saturday for the full mix.
Guatapé day trip is non-negotiable — 642 steps up La Piedra (the Rock) for the best view in Colombia.
Laureles neighbourhood for the most authentic coffee shop experience — far more local feel than tourist-heavy El Poblado.
premium.bestTimeToVisit: December–January or July–August: driest months, most events, best weather windows. The Feria de las Flores in August is unmissable.
dayTrips: Guatapé — 90 min by bus, enormous granite monolith (El Peñol) to climb, colourful lakeside village, stunning reservoir views.
Santa Fe de Antioquia — 1.5 hours northwest, colonial town, cobblestone streets, whitewashed buildings, excellent weekend escape.
Jardín — 4 hours south, coffee-growing village, humming with hummingbirds, horse riding, one of Colombia's most beautiful towns.
visaInfo: Citizens of the US, UK, EU countries, Canada, and Australia: no visa required for tourist stays up to 90 days on arrival
Colombia grants 90-day tourist permits extendable to 180 days total by visiting a Migración Colombia office
A return or onward ticket is often requested at airline check-in — book a flexible or refundable return to present if asked
Yellow fever vaccination certificate recommended and may be required if continuing to other parts of Latin America from Colombia
Current US/UK travel advisories: Medellín's tourist neighbourhoods (El Poblado, Laureles, Envigado) are considered safe; exercise standard caution and avoid displaying valuables
currencyTips: Cards are widely accepted in El Poblado's restaurants, cafés, and hostels; cash essential for local buses (buses only), street food, and markets
Best ATMs: Bancolombia and Davivienda are the most reliable for foreign cards in Medellín; withdrawal limits are 400,000–600,000 COP per transaction
Medellín's El Poblado and Laureles neighbourhoods are Colombia's most card-friendly areas — better than Bogotá or the coast for cashless payments
Avoid using ATMs on the street at night; use indoor mall or bank-branch ATMs
premium.simCard: Claro Colombia and Tigo both have solid 4G coverage in Medellín and the surrounding Antioquia region. Tourist SIMs are available at José María Córdova Airport (MDE) and at Claro/Tigo shops in the city. A 30-day SIM with 10–20 GB costs COP 40,000–70,000. eSIM via Airalo avoids the ID registration requirement for local SIMs.
